summary: Russian ballet impresario and founder of the Ballets Russes (1909, Paris), which became the most influential ballet company of the early 20th century. Famous for commissioning Stravinsky's The Firebird, Petrushka, and The Rite of Spring, employing Vaslav Nijinsky, Anna Pavlova, Tamara Karsavina, Michel Fokine, and Leonide Massine, and hiring artists like Picasso, Bakst, Benois, Goncharova, and Chanel to design sets and costumes. A flamboyantly gay impresario whose lovers included Nijinsky and Massine. Co-founded the journal Mir iskusstva (World of Art). His death in 1929 ended the Ballets Russes entirely.

works.name: The Rite of Spring (Le Sacre du printemps, 1913)
works.description: Diaghilev's most famous commission. Music by Igor Stravinsky, choreography by Vaslav Nijinsky, costumes by Nicholas Roerich. Depicts a pagan sacrifice of a virgin in Bronze Age Russia; its 1913 Paris premiere sparked a near-riot. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Commissioned by Diaghilev with music by Stravinsky and choreography by Nijinsky; caused a riot/near-riot at its 1913 Paris premiere
examples: This work was commissioned by Sergei Diaghilev, whose production with choreography by Vaslav Nijinsky started riots in the audience at a Paris premiere.
Vaslav Nijinsky choreographed this ballet, which Sergei Diaghilev staged with the Ballet Russe.

clues.clue: Depicts the sacrifice of a virgin / "Chosen One" dancing herself to death in Bronze Age / pagan Russia
examples: this controversial ballet with music by Igor Stravinsky that depicts the sacrifice of a virgin in Bronze Age Russia
Diaghilev's most controversial ballet was this work, which depicts a young girl dancing herself to death.

clues.clue: Nicholas Roerich designed long-braided Slavic folk costumes; 1913 succes de scandale
examples: Nicholas Roerich designed long-braided costumes for this ballet to more accurately depict a khorovod.
This group put on a 1913 succes de scandale in Paris for which Nicolas Roerich designed ancient Slavic folk dresses.

clues.clue: Divided into "The Adoration of the Earth" and "The Sacrifice"; opens with a famous high bassoon solo
examples: name this modernist ballet divided into "The Adoration of the Earth" and "The Sacrifice," composed by Igor Stravinsky
Camille Saint-Saens said "if that's a bassoon, I'm a baboon" in response to the high solo bassoon that opens this ballet's first act

works.name: The Firebird (L'oiseau de feu, 1910)
works.description: The 1910 ballet that launched Stravinsky's career — Diaghilev hired the unknown composer after being impressed by his Scherzo fantastique / Fireworks. Choreography by Michel Fokine; Prince Ivan uses the Firebird's feather to destroy the egg containing the soul of Koschei the Immortal. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Diaghilev launched Stravinsky's career by hiring him to compose music for The Firebird; Stravinsky's first ballet
examples: he launched another collaborator's career by hiring him to compose music for The Firebird
This ballet's original production, with choreography by Michel Fokine, began its composer's partnership with Sergei Diaghilev's Ballets Russes.

clues.clue: Diaghilev commissioned it after being impressed by Stravinsky's Scherzo fantastique / Fireworks
examples: after the Scherzo's premiere, received a commission from Sergei Diaghilev for the ballet The Firebird
Diaghilev commissioned The Firebird after being impressed by Stravinsky's short scherzo depicting these things [fireworks]

clues.clue: Prince Ivan destroys an egg containing the soul of Koschei the Immortal using the Firebird's feather; Infernal Dance
examples: Ivan Tsarevich summons the title character with her feather to shatter an egg, destroying Kashchei the Immortal
forces a group of soldiers to do an "Infernal Dance"

clues.clue: Michel Fokine choreographed the original; a 1926 Ballets Russes revival had costumes by Natalia Goncharova; a 1949 Balanchine production had costumes by Marc Chagall
examples: this artist created the costumes for the 1926 Ballet Russes revival of The Firebird
A 1949 George Balanchine production of this ballet with costumes by Marc Chagall was the breakout role of Maria Tallchief.

works.name: The Afternoon of a Faun (L'Apres-midi d'un faune, 1912)
works.description: 12-minute ballet to Debussy's Prelude. Diaghilev gave his lover Nijinsky the choreography assignment instead of Michel Fokine, causing Fokine to leave the Ballets Russes. Notorious for Nijinsky miming masturbation with a nymph's scarf at its end. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Diaghilev assigned choreography to Nijinsky instead of Fokine, which caused Fokine to leave the Ballets Russes
examples: This flamboyantly gay impresario gave his lover Vaslav Nijinsky the job of choreographing Afternoon of a Faun instead of Michel Fokine, who left this man's Ballets Russes company in response.
Fokine left this company when Diaghilev instead had Nijinsky choreograph Afternoon of a Faun.

clues.clue: Nijinsky mimed masturbation with a nymph's scarf at the end; premiere disgusted Le Figaro reviewers who called him "incontinent, with vile movements of erotic bestiality"
examples: This ballet's premiere disgusted reviewers from the newspaper Le Figaro, who called its main character "incontinent, with vile movements of erotic bestiality and gestures of heavy shamelessness."
This dancer mimed masturbating with a scarf at the end of the ballet Afternoon of a Faun

clues.clue: A 12-minute ballet in which Nijinsky made tremendous leaps Marie Rambert described as "near the stars"; notorious tight unitard with brown patches
examples: Name this 12-minute-long ballet. At its premiere, Vaslav Nijinsky made a number of tremendous leaps that Marie Rambert described as "near the stars."
A notorious one of these things was a tight unitard with brown patches meant to represent a faun.

works.name: Parade (1917)
works.description: 1917 ballet choreographed and danced by Leonide Massine — a collaboration between Erik Satie (music), Jean Cocteau (libretto), and Pablo Picasso (Cubist sets and costumes, with help from Giacomo Balla on curtains). Features three impresarios futilely trying to lure spectators into their theaters; the title refers to the side-show acts that become the focus. Guillaume Apollinaire's program notes coined the term "Surrealism." Wikipedia

clues.clue: Collaboration between Erik Satie (music), Jean Cocteau, and Pablo Picasso (Cubist sets/costumes); Giacomo Balla helped with the curtains
examples: Pablo Picasso designed the set and fashioned cubist costumes for this ballet with aid from Giacomo Balla, who helped with the curtains.
this ballet, a collaboration between Erik Satie, Jean Cocteau, and Pablo Picasso

clues.clue: Choreographed and danced at its 1917 premiere by Leonide Massine
examples: Name this ballet choreographed and danced at its 1917 premiere by Leonide Massine.

clues.clue: Features three impresarios futilely trying to get people to enter their theaters; title refers to side acts that become the focus
examples: It features three impresarios futilely attempting to get people to enter their theaters, and its title refers to the side acts that end up being the focus of attention.

clues.clue: Apollinaire's program notes coined the term "Surrealism"; dancers wore boxy costumes representing buildings
examples: Guillaume Apollinaire coined the term "Surrealism" in the program notes to the ballet Parade
He designed costumes for Satie's Parade which forced dancers to wear boxy costumes representing buildings.

works.name: Petrushka (1911)
works.description: Stravinsky ballet about a puppet who comes to life. The introverted title puppet is "all turned in" while the Moor is "all turned out"; he and the Moor vie for the love of the Ballerina. Features the famous "Petrushka chord" combining clashing C major and F-sharp major triads. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Stravinsky wrote Petrushka for Diaghilev's Ballets Russes (listed with Firebird and Rite of Spring as Stravinsky's three Russian-period Diaghilev ballets)
examples: He wrote Petrushka for Sergei Diaghilev's Ballets Russes.
name this composer for ballets like Petrushka, The Firebird, and The Rite of Spring

clues.clue: Title puppet and the Moor vie for the love of the Ballerina; Nijinsky danced the title role
examples: This dancer was the first to dance the titular role in a ballet about the title character and the Moor, who vie for the love of the Ballerina.
the introverted title puppet should be all turned in

works.name: Les Noces (The Wedding, 1923)
works.description: Stravinsky ballet scored for mixed chorus, percussion, and four pianos, depicting a Russian peasant wedding. Choreographed by Bronislava Nijinska, the younger sister of Nijinsky (Diaghilev's former lover). Wikipedia

clues.clue: Choreography by Bronislava Nijinska, the younger sister of Diaghilev's lover Nijinsky (who broke with him and was diagnosed with schizophrenia)
examples: This man's collaborator on the choreography of Les Noces was the younger sister of his lover, who broke up with him in an acrimonious dispute and was diagnosed with schizophrenia.
Nijinsky's sister Bronislava choreographed this Stravinsky ballet scored for mixed chorus, percussion, and four pianos.

clues.clue: Scored for mixed chorus, percussion, and four pianos; includes tableaus "The Bridegroom's House" and "The Wedding Feast"
examples: Bronislava's somber, rigid choreography reflects a woman's obligation to marry.
a ballet that includes the tableaus "The Bridegroom's House" and "The Wedding Feast" and is titled Les noces

works.name: Les Sylphides / Chopiniana (1909)
works.description: Plotless ballet blanc re-choreographed by Michel Fokine for the first Ballets Russes season. Female spirits dance in the moonlight around a male "poet" to orchestrations of Chopin (including the Military Polonaise), originally by Glazunov. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Plotless ballet blanc re-choreographed by Michel Fokine for the first season of the Ballets Russes; female spirits dance in the moonlight
examples: Fokine re-choreographed this plotless ballet blanc for the first season of the Ballet Russes, in which female spirits dance in the moonlight.
Name this 1909 Michel Fokine ballet in which the title white-wearing spirits dance in the moonlight around a male "poet."

clues.clue: Music uses Chopin's Military Polonaise and other Chopin works orchestrated by Glazunov; renamed from Chopiniana
examples: This ballet's music uses the Military Polonaise and other works by Frederic Chopin.
renamed from its original title Chopiniana

works.name: The Good-Humored Ladies / Pulcinella / "time-travelling" adaptations
works.description: Diaghilev had a passion for pastiche adaptations of older composers, which Constant Lambert termed "time-travelling" works. The Good-Humored Ladies adapted Goldoni with 20 of Scarlatti's 555 sonatas; Pulcinella was a neoclassical ballet with music based on works spuriously attributed to Pergolesi. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Diaghilev chose 20 Scarlatti sonatas out of 555 to score an adaptation of Carlo Goldoni's The Good-Humored Ladies — the first of what Constant Lambert termed his "time-travelling" works
examples: This man went through the painstaking process of choosing twenty sonatas out of Domenico Scarlatti's 555 to score an adaptation of Carlo Goldoni's The Good-Humored Ladies, the first of what Constant Lambert termed his "time-travelling" works.

clues.clue: Pulcinella: a neoclassical ballet whose music is based on compositions spuriously attributed to Pergolesi
examples: Name this neoclassical ballet whose music is based on compositions spuriously attributed to Giovanni Pergolesi.

works.name: General / Biographical (Impresario, Ballets Russes)
works.description: How Diaghilev is most commonly identified: Russian founder of the Ballets Russes (Paris, 1909), ballet impresario, flamboyantly gay, collaborator with Stravinsky, patron of many modernist painters.

clues.clue: Russian founder of the Ballets Russes (Paris, 1909); collaborated with Stravinsky; employed Nijinsky, Fokine, Pavlova, Karsavina, Massine

This play takes its title from a line in "September 1, 1939" that reads, "What mad Nijinsky wrote / About Diaghilev / Is true of [the title concept]."

comprehensive_summary: Sergei Pavlovich Diaghilev (1872-1929) was a Russian art critic, patron, and ballet impresario who founded the Ballets Russes in Paris in 1909 and single-handedly transformed it into the most influential ballet company of the early 20th century. Before turning to dance, Diaghilev co-founded the journal Mir iskusstva (World of Art) with his friends Alexandre Benois and Leon Bakst, both of whom would later design costumes and sets for his ballets. His career as an impresario is inseparable from his discovery of Igor Stravinsky: after being impressed by Stravinsky's Scherzo fantastique and Fireworks, he commissioned the unknown composer to write The Firebird (1910), which launched one of the 20th century's most important musical careers. Diaghilev and Stravinsky would go on to collaborate on Petrushka (1911), The Rite of Spring (1913) — whose 1913 Paris premiere, choreographed by Vaslav Nijinsky with costumes by Nicholas Roerich, caused a near-riot — Les Noces (1923), and Pulcinella. Diaghilev's most famous productions are repeatedly clued by commission details: he paired Nijinsky with Debussy for The Afternoon of a Faun (1912), provoking Michel Fokine's departure from the company and a Le Figaro review that called Nijinsky "incontinent, with vile movements of erotic bestiality." He produced Parade (1917), a landmark collaboration between Erik Satie, Jean Cocteau, Pablo Picasso (Cubist costumes), and Leonide Massine that Guillaume Apollinaire's program notes dubbed "Surrealist." A flamboyantly gay man whose major lovers included Nijinsky and Massine, Diaghilev fired Nijinsky after Nijinsky's marriage to the Hungarian stalker Romola de Pulszky and reportedly "gave himself to a wild orgy of dissipation." He was the inspiration for Boris Lermontov in Powell and Pressburger's The Red Shoes. Diaghilev had a voracious appetite for commissioning modernist artists: Natalia Goncharova, Mikhail Larionov, Sonia Delaunay, Coco Chanel, Joan Miro, Giorgio de Chirico, Andre Derain, and Matisse all designed for him. His commissions ranged across composers from Stravinsky to Prokofiev (The Prodigal Son; he rejected the Scythian Suite) to Manuel de Falla (The Three-Cornered Hat), Reynaldo Hahn (Le dieu bleu), Respighi (La Boutique fantasque), and Germaine Tailleferre (La nouvelle Cythere, never performed because Diaghilev's 1929 death cancelled the whole season). Constant Lambert termed his Scarlatti/Goldoni adaptation The Good-Humored Ladies the first of his "time-travelling" pastiche works. Diaghilev famously quarrelled with Maurice Ravel over La valse, refusing to choreograph it — Ravel challenged him to a duel. In the world of literature, W. H. Auden's poem "September 1, 1939" immortalized the line "What mad Nijinsky wrote / About Diaghilev / Is true of the normal heart," and Larry Kramer borrowed from it for the title of his AIDS play The Normal Heart; Frank Bidart also made him a subject in "The War of Vaslav Nijinsky."
recursive_suggestions: Ballets Russes — frequently its own answerline (e.g. 2023 Chicago Open); the company Diaghilev founded
Vaslav Nijinsky — Diaghilev's lover, principal choreographer, huge cross-ref with Afternoon of a Faun / Rite of Spring
The Rite of Spring — own answerline many times; most heavily clued Diaghilev production
The Firebird — own answerline several times; the Stravinsky ballet that launched his career
Igor Stravinsky — Diaghilev's most important collaborator
Michel Fokine — choreographer of Les Sylphides, Firebird, The Dying Swan; left the company over Faun
Leonide Massine — Nijinsky's successor and Diaghilev's later lover
Natalia Goncharova — Russian modernist painter who designed Ballets Russes costumes
Anna Pavlova — Ballets Russes star ballerina
"September 1, 1939" (Auden) — literary cross-ref from "What mad Nijinsky wrote about Diaghilev"
